content:
Why unordered_map<pair<int, int>, int> Doesn't Compile in C++
A few days ago, I was solving a DSA problem where I wanted to use a pair<int, int> as the key in an unordered_map.
Naturally, I wrote:
unordered_map<pair<int, int>, int> mp;I expected it to compile because this works without any issues:
map<pair<int, int>, int> mp;Instead, the compiler threw a long error message. At first, I thought I had made a syntax mistake, but the real reason is much more interesting.
Let’s see why this happens.
Why does map work?
std::map stores elements in sorted order. To keep the elements sorted, it only needs a way to compare two keys.
Fortunately, std::pair already supports comparison.
pair<int, int> a = {1, 2};
pair<int, int> b = {1, 3};cout << (a < b); // trueThat’s enough for map.
map<pair<int, int>, int> mp;No extra work is required.
unordered_map works completely differently.
Instead of comparing keys, it stores them in buckets. To decide which bucket a key belongs to, it first computes a hash value.
The process looks like this:
Key
│
▼
Hash Function
│
▼
Hash Value
│
▼
BucketSo whenever you create an unordered_map, the container must know how to hash the key.
For an int, that's easy because the standard library already provides:
std::hash<int>For a string, it provides:
std::hash<string>But for pair<int, int>?
There isn’t one.
That’s exactly why this doesn’t compile:
unordered_map<pair<int, int>, int> mp;The compiler tries to find
std::hash<pair<int, int>>but it doesn’t exist in the standard library.
How do we fix it?
We simply provide our own hash function.
#include <iostream>
#include <unordered_map>using namespace std;struct PairHash { //convert a pair<int,int> into a single hash value
size_t operator()(const pair<int, int>& p) const {
return hash<int>()(p.first) ^
(hash<int>()(p.second) << 1);
}
};int main() {
unordered_map<pair<int, int>, int, PairHash> mp; mp[{1, 2}] = 10;
mp[{3, 4}] = 20; cout << mp[{1, 2}] << '\n';
}Now the compiler knows how to generate a hash value for every pair.
Why do we combine the hashes?
A pair contains two values, but unordered_map needs a single hash value.
One simple approach is:
hash<int>()(p.first) ^
(hash<int>()(p.second) << 1)Here we hash both integers separately and combine them using XOR (^).
There are more advanced hash-combining techniques, but this one is good enough for most competitive programming and interview problems.
A common misconception
Many people think unordered_map should automatically support pair because pair is part of the standard library.
That sounds reasonable, but unordered_map doesn't just need a key type—it needs a way to hash that key. Since the standard library doesn't provide a default hash for std::pair, you have to provide one yourself.
Final thoughts
This is one of those compiler errors that looks scary at first but has a simple explanation.

• map needs comparison.

• pair already supports comparison.

• unordered_map needs hashing.

• pair doesn't have a default hash.

• Writing a custom hash solves the problem.
Once you understand this difference, the error message suddenly makes a lot more sense.
